summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – Practice and procedure – Application for stay pending appeal – Where the applicant seeks to stay order calling upon his litigation guardian to show cause why she should not be removed – Order stayed pending disposition of appeal. FAMILY LAW – Practice and procedure – Application for disqualification – Where the applicant makes an application for disqualification on the basis of bias or apprehension of bias – Where the applicant does not establish any basis for disqualification – Wife’s application for disqualification dismissed.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

JARRETT J:

On 5 March 2026 I made an order dismissing an application by the litigation guardian for the applicant for an order under s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth). Further, I ordered that the applicant’s litigation guardian, Ms B Bongiovani, show cause at 9.30 am on 13 April 2026 why:
(a) she should not be removed as a litigation guardian for the applicant; or alternatively

(b) why the proceedings should not be stayed until such time as legal representation has been engaged and instructed for the applicant.

Although the notice of appeal is not in evidence before me, Ms B Bongiovani has sought leave to appeal both of those orders by a Notice of Appeal filed on 16 March 2026.
By her Application in a Proceeding filed on 16 March 2026, she now seeks:
(a) pending the outcome of her appeal, a stay of the order requiring her to show cause why she should not be removed as the applicant’s litigation guardian; and

(b) an order that I disqualify myself from “further dealing with these Proceedings”.

The applicant, by his litigation guardian, seemed to have no difficulty with me first deciding the stay application before then going on to determine if I should disqualify myself.

The stay application

Whilst the respondent is entitled to the benefit of the order I have made (in the broadest sense given the nature of the order under appeal) and that order is presumed to be correct, if Ms B Bongiovani establishes a proper basis for the stay, the court may stay the operation of the order under appeal. It is not necessary for the applicant to demonstrate any “special” or “exceptional” circumstances to secure the stay. Taking into account the bona fides of the appeal, the balance of convenience and the competing rights of the parties, the risk that an appeal may be rendered nugatory if a stay is not granted, some preliminary assessment of the strength of the appeal and the practical arrangements for the hearing of the appeal (such as when it might be heard) , the court may grant the stay if it is satisfied that it is appropriate to do so.
I am satisfied that the appeal is bona fide. If the stay is not granted, the appeal might be nugatory because the applicant’s litigation guardian may have been removed. She could, of course, be re-appointed in the event of a successful appeal, but there seems to be little advantage to such a course. The respondent points to no prejudice arising from a delay in dealing with the show cause procedure other than a lapse of time. On that point, having made enquiries, I am informed that the appeal is likely to be listed for hearing before a Full Court in the June sittings. Accordingly, the delay until the hearing of the appeal is relatively short.
As to the merits of the appeal, the basis upon which I determined that the applicant’s litigation guardian should show cause why she should not be removed are set out in my reasons delivered on 5 March 2026. In his proposed Notice of Appeal, the applicant particularises two findings said to be erroneous, namely:
(a) “The evidence provided by the Applicant was sufficient and as such she was wrongly deemed incompetent”.

(b) “The evidence regarding the intentions of Judicial Registrar Brooks allowing the Litigation Guardian to act at the trial in the event that a solicitor was not engaged was quite clear.”

As to the first matter, my reasons show that I did not consider that the application for an order pursuant to s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) was sufficient to secure that order. I described the application as “hopeless” for that reason. As to the litigation guardian’s competence, I said at [14]:
The evidence used to support it was entirely inadequate but, yet, proper legal advice and proper legal representation might have seen a different outcome, although I suspect not, but one never knows. The applicant in the proceedings, the person for whom Ms [B Bongiovani] acts as litigation guardian, has been denied the opportunity of having a successful outcome on that application because of her incompetence.
The only evidence relied upon by the applicant was an affidavit by the litigation guardian in which she swore “I can confirm that the Applicant has been the subject to domestic abuse by the Respondent Wife”. An Application for an Apprehended Domestic Violence Order was annexed to the affidavit, but the application was not signed and its contents neither sworn nor sworn to.
The office of litigation guardian (or next friend, tutor or guardian ad litem depending upon the era, court and role) is ancient. A litigation guardian is an officer of the court and the duties of that office are described in Daniell’s Chancery Practice (Sweet & Maxwell, 8th ed, 1985) at pages 100 and 101. Moreover, the duties and responsibilities of a litigation guardian (or someone in a concomitant role) were described in F, M v Attorney-General's Department [2013] SADC 123, particularly at [24].
The second matter, said to be a finding, was that a judicial registrar had apparently authorised the litigation guardian to appear in court to represent the applicant. However, the order appointing the litigation guardian makes no such provision. Whatever statements may have been made by the judicial registrar or those that assist her, the position at law, generally speaking, is that a litigation guardian must act under legal advice and is not entitled to appear personally before the court (see F, M v Attorney-General's Department at [24] and the authorities there cited).
Moreover, by s 57 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth) a party to a proceeding before this court is not entitled to be represented by another person unless:
(a) under the Judiciary Act 1903, the other person is entitled to practise as a barrister or solicitor, or both, in a federal court; or

(b) under the regulations, the other person is taken to be an authorised representative; or

(c) another law of the Commonwealth authorises the other person to represent the party.

The litigation guardian is not a legal practitioner. There are no regulations that confer a right of audience upon her and the general law is that a litigation guardian must appear by a solicitor.
I cannot conclude that the applicant’s prospects of securing leave to appeal and then a successful appeal are strong. They are not non-existent, but it seems to me that the proposed appeal is not strong.
Nonetheless, taking into account the bona fides of the appeal, the short period until the appeal is likely to be heard, the nature of the order under appeal and the absence of any likely prejudice to the respondent, I consider that it is appropriate to grant a stay of order 2 made on 5 March 2026 until the determination of the applicant’s appeal against that order.

Disqualification

In Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy [2000] HCA 63; (2000) 205 CLR 337 the plurality of the High Court of Australia said (at [6]):
... a judge is disqualified if a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the judge might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question the judge is required to decide. That principle gives effect to the requirement that justice should both be done and be seen to be done, a requirement which reflects the fundamental importance of the principle that the tribunal be independent and impartial. It is convenient to refer to it as the apprehension of bias principle.
(Footnotes omitted)
The question is whether a fair-minded lay observer might reasonably apprehend that the decision-maker might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the issue they are to decide. The answer to the question involves a two-step analysis.
The first step is to identify what it is said might lead a decision maker to decide a case other than on its legal and factual merits (Ebner at [8], Henley & Bestari (2024) FLC 94–169 at [25]).
As to this, in an affidavit in support of the application, the litigation guardian suggests that at a directions hearing on 20 January 2026, I informed the litigation guardian that she could not appear for the applicant at the final hearing. Although there is no transcript of the hearing before me, I accept that I probably said such a thing. The litigation guardian says that I raised my voice with her and “continually belittled me”. She gives no particulars of what it is that I am said to have said to her, other than that she could not appear for the applicant at the final hearing.
The applicant next complains that I did not consider all of the material she put before me on the application for the s 102NA(1)(c)(iv) application. Whether or not I did is a matter for the appeal against my dismissal of that order. I do not consider that it is a matter that goes to the question now under consideration.
The litigation guardian swears that on 3 February 2026, at the hearing of the application of the s 102NA order, the respondent’s solicitor suggested that Ms B Bongiovani should not be the litigation guardian for the applicant. She suggests that I agreed. There is no transcript of the exchange. She swears, “This was awful. These 2 were talking that way knowing I would find it offensive and knowing the Applicant would find it very upsetting.” There are no particulars.
The litigation guardian further swears that I said that I would reserve my decision on the application. But, she says, “Such Application clearly stated that it will be heard in an open Court but this never occurred as HH made His decision in chambers. In doing so I was never given a chance to expand on my Affidavit if need be”. None of this, however, is correct. The application was heard in open court and both parties were given the opportunity to make submissions in support of their respective positions. I reserved my decision. Something which is entirely orthodox. I delivered my decision in open court in the presence of the parties on 5 March 2026. These statements by the litigation guardian demonstrate a basic misunderstanding of procedure.
The litigation guardian makes other assertions in her affidavit about various matters, none of which have any particularity.
I do not consider that the applicant has identified any matter that is said might lead me to decide this case other than on its legal and factual merits.
Even if I am wrong about that and any of the matters referred to above, taken either separately or as a whole, satisfy the first step of the test, the applicant does not demonstrate a logical connection between those matters and the feared departure from deciding the case on its merits. The applicant’s litigation guardian feels aggrieved by my decision on the s 102NA application and complains about my manner but draws no connection between them and the real possibility that I might not bring an impartial mind to the resolution of the question I am likely to be required to decide.
The application that I disqualify myself from further involvement in these proceedings will be dismissed.
In the course of preparation of these reasons, it has become apparent that there is an error on the cover sheet of the reasons for judgment delivered on 5 March 2026. That sheet records the date of hearing as 5 March 2026. The hearing in fact occurred on 3 February 2026 and the cover sheet should be amended accordingly.
